era_context: The French Revolution's radical phase has ended; the Directory (1795–1799) is in power but facing military defeats and internal corruption. The Napoleonic Wars are beginning. The Qianlong Emperor of Qing China is in his final years (abdicated 1796, died 1799). The Ottoman Empire continues its slow decline. The United States is in the presidency of John Adams (1797–1801). The Industrial Revolution accelerates in Britain.

events.title: Treaty of Campo Formio
events.description: Signed on 17 October 1797, the Treaty of Campo Formio ended the War of the First Coalition between France and Austria. The treaty was negotiated by Napoleon and Austrian representatives and resulted in significant French territorial gains. France retained control of the left bank of the Rhine and gained the Ionian Islands. Austria ceded the Austrian Netherlands (modern Belgium) to France and received Venice and its territories in compensation. The treaty marked a major French victory and enhanced Napoleon's prestige. However, it also created resentment among other European powers, particularly Britain and Russia, setting the stage for the Second Coalition War.

sources.name: Treaty of Campo Formio, signed document
Schroeder, P.W., The Transformation of European Politics 1763–1848

events.certainty_note: Treaty date and terms are well-documented in diplomatic archives.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor abdicates in favor of Jiaqing Emperor
events.description: In 1797, the Qianlong Emperor, one of the greatest Qing rulers, formally abdicated in favor of his son, the Jiaqing Emperor. However, Qianlong retained power as the 'Retired Emperor' until his death in 1799. The abdication was intended to follow the precedent of Kangxi, who had also abdicated after a long reign. However, Qianlong's continued influence created a complex power structure in which both the Jiaqing Emperor and the Retired Emperor exercised authority. The eunuch Heshen, who had dominated court politics under Qianlong, continued to wield significant influence.

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
Spence, J.D., Search for Modern China

events.certainty_note: Abdication date is well-documented in Qing records.

events.title: Battle of Cape St. Vincent
events.description: Fought on 14 February 1797, the Battle of Cape St. Vincent was a British naval victory under Admiral John Jervis against the Spanish fleet. The battle was fought off the coast of Portugal and resulted in the capture of two Spanish ships. The British victory prevented a Spanish invasion of Britain and demonstrated British naval superiority. The battle was significant in establishing British naval dominance during the Napoleonic Wars and contributed to the eventual British victory in the war.

sources.name: Mahan, A.T., The Influence of Sea Power upon the French Revolution and Empire

events.certainty_note: Battle date and outcome are well-documented in naval records.

events.title: Mutiny at Spithead and the Nore
events.description: In 1797, British naval sailors mutinied at Spithead (April–May) and the Nore (May–June) over poor pay, harsh conditions, and lack of shore leave. The mutinies were the largest naval mutinies in British history and posed a serious threat to British naval power during the Napoleonic Wars. The mutineers' demands included better pay, improved food, and better medical care. The government eventually conceded to most of the demands, and the mutinies were suppressed. The mutinies demonstrated the vulnerability of British naval power and the importance of maintaining sailor morale.

sources.name: British naval records and contemporary accounts
Rodger, N.A.M., The Command of the Ocean: A Naval History of Britain 1649–1815

events.certainty_note: Mutiny dates and outcomes are well-documented in naval records.

events.title: John Adams inaugurated as second U.S. President
events.description: John Adams was inaugurated as the second President of the United States on 4 March 1797, following the retirement of George Washington. Adams, a Federalist, faced significant challenges including tensions with France over the Jay Treaty (1794) and domestic political divisions between Federalists and Republicans. Adams' presidency was marked by the Quasi-War with France (1798–1800) and efforts to maintain American neutrality during the Napoleonic Wars. Adams' presidency also saw the passage of the Alien and Sedition Acts (1798), which were controversial and contributed to his defeat in the 1800 election.

sources.name: Ellis, J.J., Founding Brothers: The Revolutionary Generation

events.certainty_note: Inauguration date and contemporary records are unambiguous.

events.title: XYZ Affair — U.S.-French diplomatic crisis
events.description: The XYZ Affair was a diplomatic crisis between the United States and France in 1797–1798. American diplomats sent to negotiate with France were approached by French officials (referred to as X, Y, and Z in official reports) who demanded bribes and loans before negotiations could begin. The American diplomats refused and reported the incident to President Adams. The affair outraged American public opinion and contributed to the Quasi-War between the United States and France (1798–1800). The incident demonstrated the vulnerability of American diplomacy and the need for a strong military.

sources.name: DeConde, A., The Quasi-War: The Politics and Diplomacy of the Undeclared War with France

events.certainty_note: XYZ Affair dates and details are well-documented in diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Tipu Sultan's death and the end of the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War
events.description: Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore and a major opponent of British expansion in India, died on 4 May 1799 (though the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War ended in 1799, the events leading to it occurred in 1797–1798). Tipu Sultan had allied with France against the British and sought to modernize Mysore's military. His death marked the end of significant Indian resistance to British expansion in southern India. The British victory in the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1799) consolidated British control over southern India and paved the way for British dominance of the Indian subcontinent.

sources.name: British military records and Indian sources
Dalrymple, W., The Anarchy: The Relentless Rise of the East India Company

events.certainty_note: Tipu Sultan's death date is well-documented, though the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War formally ended in 1799.

events.title: Romantic movement in literature — emergence
events.description: The Romantic movement in literature was emerging in the late 1790s, with poets and writers beginning to emphasize emotion, nature, individualism, and the imagination, departing from the rationalism and formalism of the Enlightenment. Key figures included William Wordsworth, Samuel Taylor Coleridge, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, and others. The movement would reach its full expression in the early 19th century with works like Lyrical Ballads (1798) and the poetry of Byron, Keats, and Shelley.

sources.name: Abrams, M.H., The Mirror and the Lamp: Romantic Theory and the Critical Tradition

events.certainty_note: The emergence of Romanticism is a gradual process; 1797 is part of this emergence but not a discrete event.

events.title: Goethe's Faust (Part I) — publication
events.description: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's Faust (Part I) was published in 1797, though it had been in development for many years. The work is a dramatic poem based on the legend of Faust, a scholar who makes a pact with the devil. Faust is considered one of the greatest works of German literature and a masterpiece of the Romantic movement. The work explores themes of ambition, knowledge, love, and redemption. Goethe's Faust had a profound influence on subsequent European literature and thought.

sources.name: Boyle, N., Goethe: The Poet and the Age

events.certainty_note: Publication date and contents are well-documented in literary records.

events.title: Ottoman Empire — ongoing decline and military weakness
events.description: In 1797, the Ottoman Empire continued its slow decline, facing military defeats, internal corruption, and economic stagnation. The empire's inability to modernize its military and administration left it vulnerable to European powers. The Treaty of Campo Formio (1797) between France and Austria, which did not include the Ottoman Empire, demonstrated the empire's marginalization in European affairs. The empire's decline would accelerate in the 19th century, leading to its eventual dissolution after World War I.

sources.name: Ottoman diplomatic records and European accounts
Shaw, S.J., History of the Ottoman Empire and Modern Turkey

events.certainty_note: Ottoman decline is a long-term process; 1797 is not a discrete event but part of a broader trajectory.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Campo Formio is sometimes presented as a complete French victory, but modern historians emphasize that it was a compromise that satisfied neither France nor Austria. The treaty's provision giving Venice to Austria was controversial and contributed to later conflicts. The Qianlong Emperor's abdication is sometimes presented as a straightforward succession, but modern historians emphasize that it created a complex power structure in which both the Qianlong Emperor and the Jiaqing Emperor exercised authority, leading to confusion and inefficiency. The Battle of Cape St. Vincent is sometimes presented as a decisive British victory, but the Spanish fleet escaped and was not destroyed. The XYZ Affair is sometimes presented as a straightforward bribery attempt, but modern historians emphasize that it was a complex diplomatic negotiation in which both sides had legitimate grievances.
historiographic_note: The year 1797 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly British, French, and Austrian archives. The documentation is heavily weighted toward political and military events in Europe and North America. Events in Asia, Africa, and the Ottoman Empire are less thoroughly recorded in Western sources. The Qing records are rich but less accessible to Western scholarship. The perspective is shaped by the dominance of Napoleonic history in European historiography. The Treaty of Campo Formio is often presented from the French perspective; Austrian and Italian perspectives are less well-represented. The XYZ Affair is often presented from the American perspective; French perspectives are less well-represented.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Campo Formio's territorial changes contributed to formation of Second Coalition
Qianlong's abdication led to his death in 1799 and Jiaqing's consolidation
Battle of Cape St. Vincent was part of British naval dominance during Napoleonic Wars
Naval mutinies occurred shortly after Battle of Cape St. Vincent
Adams' presidency led to his defeat in 1800 election
XYZ Affair contributed to escalation of Quasi-War in 1798
Tipu Sultan's opposition to British led to Fourth Anglo-Mysore War in 1799
Romantic movement emergence led to Lyrical Ballads publication in 1798
Goethe's Faust was part of Romantic movement emergence

era_context: The late 18th century, dominated by the aftermath of the French Revolution and the rise of Napoleon Bonaparte. Europe is in flux: the French Republic is consolidating power, monarchies are threatened, and colonial empires are competing globally. The Industrial Revolution is accelerating in Britain. The Americas are establishing new republics following independence movements.

events.title: Napoleon's Italian Campaign begins
events.description: General Napoleon Bonaparte launches his first major military campaign, commanding the French Army of Italy against the Austro-Piedmontese alliance. Between April and November 1796, he executes a series of rapid maneuvers that separate the Austrian and Piedmontese armies, defeating them in detail at Montenotte, Lodi, and other engagements. This campaign establishes Napoleon's reputation as a military genius and results in the Treaty of Paris (May 1796) forcing Piedmont out of the war. The campaign demonstrates the effectiveness of concentrated force and rapid movement, reshaping European military doctrine.
key_figures: Field Marshal Dagobert Sigmund von Wurmser (Austrian commander)

sources.name: Napoleon's own dispatches and memoirs (Mémorial de Sainte-Hélène)
Historiographic consensus in military histories (Chandler, Asprey, Markham)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary military records, dispatches, and later memoirs. Dates and outcomes are uncontested.

events.title: Edward Jenner's first smallpox vaccination
events.description: On 14 May 1796, English physician Edward Jenner inoculates eight-year-old James Phipps with material from a cowpox pustule obtained from milkmaid Sarah Nelmes. Phipps develops a mild illness and subsequently proves immune to smallpox inoculation. This experiment, conducted on Jenner's farm near Berkeley, marks the birth of vaccination as a systematic medical practice. Jenner's work, published in 1798 as 'An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae,' provides the foundation for the global eradication of smallpox two centuries later.
key_figures: Sarah Nelmes (milkmaid, source of cowpox material)

sources.name: Jenner's own account and later publication 'An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae' (1798)
Medical historiography and archival records at the Jenner Museum

events.certainty_note: The date of 14 May 1796 is well-established in Jenner's own records and subsequent publications. The experiment is one of the most thoroughly documented moments in medical history.

events.title: British naval victory at Cape St. Vincent
events.region: Off Cape St. Vincent, southwestern Spain
events.description: On 14 February 1796, a British fleet under Admiral Sir John Jervis defeats a larger Spanish fleet off Cape St. Vincent. The British, numbering 15 ships of the line, engage a Spanish fleet of 27 ships. Through superior tactics and seamanship, Jervis's fleet inflicts heavy casualties and captures four Spanish ships without losing any. The victory, achieved partly through the aggressive tactics of Commodore Nelson, boosts British naval morale during a period of naval strain and prevents Spanish reinforcement of French forces in the Mediterranean.

sources.name: British Admiralty records and official dispatches
Spanish naval records and later histories

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in British and Spanish naval records. The date and outcome are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader Anglo-Spanish naval conflict (1796-1808)
Led to Nelson's rise to prominence

events.title: British occupation of the Cape of Good Hope
events.region: Cape of Good Hope, South Africa
events.description: In September 1796, a British naval and military expedition under Admiral Sir George Elphinstone and General James Henry Craig captures the Cape of Good Hope from the Dutch. The Dutch had held the Cape since 1652 as a crucial supply station on the route to Asia. The British, seeking to deny the French access to this strategic point and to secure their own eastern trade routes, conduct a brief campaign and occupy the settlement. The Cape remains under British control (with brief interruptions) until 1961, becoming a cornerstone of British imperial strategy in the Indian Ocean.
key_figures: Admiral Sir George Elphinstone (British naval commander)
General James Henry Craig (British military commander)

sources.name: British Admiralty and War Office records

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in British and Dutch records. The date and strategic significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader British imperial expansion
Led to long-term British control of southern Africa

events.title: Whiskey Rebellion aftermath and consolidation of U.S. federal authority
events.description: By 1796, the aftermath of the Whiskey Rebellion (1794) has solidified federal authority in the young United States. The successful suppression of the rebellion by federal troops under President Washington demonstrated that the new federal government could enforce its laws and taxation. In 1796, this consolidation is reflected in the relative quiet on the frontier and the acceptance of federal excise taxes. The rebellion's suppression marks a turning point in American federalism, establishing the principle that the federal government, not state governments or local militias, holds ultimate coercive power.
key_figures: Alexander Hamilton (Secretary of Treasury, architect of the excise tax)
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of federal authority following the rebellion is well-documented. 1796 marks the point at which the rebellion's lessons had been absorbed into American political practice.
cross_references: Part of broader development of U.S. federalism

events.title: George Washington's Farewell Address
events.description: On 17 September 1796, President George Washington publishes his Farewell Address, announcing his intention not to seek a third term and offering advice to the nation. The address, drafted with input from Alexander Hamilton and James Madison, warns against the dangers of political parties, sectionalism, and foreign entanglements. The phrase 'entangling alliances' becomes a cornerstone of American foreign policy for generations. Washington's voluntary relinquishment of power sets a precedent for peaceful transfer of executive authority and establishes the two-term tradition (later codified in the 22nd Amendment).

sources.name: The Farewell Address, published in newspapers and later compilations
Washington's personal papers and correspondence with Hamilton and Madison

events.certainty_note: The date of publication (17 September 1796) is well-established. The text and its authorship are thoroughly documented.

events.title: U.S. Presidential election of 1796
events.description: In the autumn of 1796, the United States holds its second presidential election. John Adams, the Federalist candidate and sitting Vice President, defeats Thomas Jefferson, the Democratic-Republican candidate. Adams receives 71 electoral votes to Jefferson's 68. The election marks the first contested presidential election and the first peaceful transfer of power from one political faction to another. Jefferson, as the runner-up, becomes Vice President under the electoral system then in place. The election reflects growing partisan divisions between Federalists (favoring strong central government and commercial interests) and Democratic-Republicans (favoring agrarian interests and state power).
key_figures: Thomas Jefferson (Democratic-Republican candidate, elected Vice President)

sources.name: Personal correspondence of Adams, Jefferson, Hamilton, and Madison

events.certainty_note: Electoral results and dates are thoroughly documented in official records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Russian advances in the Caucasus
events.description: During 1796, Russian forces under General Grigory Potemkin's successors continue operations against the Ottoman Empire in the Caucasus and along the Black Sea. Russian expansion into the Caucasus, which had begun in the 1760s, accelerates. The Russians capture several Ottoman fortresses and extend their influence over the region. These campaigns are part of the broader Russo-Turkish Wars (1768-1774, 1787-1792, 1806-1812) and represent Russia's systematic expansion southward at Ottoman expense. By 1796, Russia has become the dominant power in the northern Caucasus.
key_figures: General Grigory Potemkin (deceased 1791, but his strategic legacy continues)
events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Russian-Ottoman conflict in 1796 are well-documented, though specific battles and dates may vary slightly in different sources. The strategic direction is clear.
cross_references: Led to Russian consolidation in the Caucasus

events.title: Tipu Sultan's death and fall of Mysore
events.description: On 4 May 1799 (not 1796, but the events leading to this outcome occur in 1796), Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore, is killed in the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War. However, in 1796, Tipu is still actively resisting British expansion. The Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1799) is preceded by years of tension and skirmishing. In 1796, Tipu is consolidating his power and attempting to modernize Mysore's military with French assistance. The eventual fall of Mysore in 1799 marks the end of Indian resistance to British expansion in southern India and the beginning of British dominance in the subcontinent.

sources.name: British East India Company records and military dispatches

events.certainty_note: The events of 1796 are part of the lead-up to the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1799). The specific events of 1796 are less well-documented than the war itself, but the general trajectory is clear.
cross_references: Led to 1799-001 (Fourth Anglo-Mysore War and Tipu Sultan's death)
Part of broader British expansion in India

events.title: Chinese Qing Dynasty: Qianlong Emperor's abdication
events.description: In 1796, the Qianlong Emperor of the Qing Dynasty formally abdicates in favor of his son, the Jiaqing Emperor, though Qianlong retains significant power as the 'Retired Emperor' (Taishang Huang) until his death in 1799. The abdication is partly motivated by Qianlong's desire not to rule longer than his grandfather, the Kangxi Emperor. However, the transition marks the beginning of the Qing Dynasty's gradual decline. By 1796, the Qing has already begun to face internal challenges: the White Lotus Rebellion (1796-1804) erupts during this period, signaling growing social unrest and the weakening of central authority.
key_figures: Heshen (powerful official and favorite of Qianlong)

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Qing Shilu) and court documents
Chinese historical chronicles and official histories
Contemporary European diplomatic reports from China

events.certainty_note: The abdication of Qianlong in 1796 is well-documented in Qing imperial records. The date and circumstances are uncontested.

events.title: White Lotus Rebellion begins in China
events.description: In 1796, the White Lotus Rebellion erupts in central China, beginning in Hubei Province and spreading to Sichuan and Shaanxi. The rebellion, led by members of the White Lotus sect (a syncretic Buddhist-Daoist religious movement), is motivated by economic hardship, resentment of Qing taxation, and messianic religious beliefs. The rebellion lasts until 1804 and represents one of the most serious challenges to Qing authority in the 18th century. The uprising demonstrates the weakening of Qing military effectiveness and the growing social instability that will characterize the 19th century. The rebellion is suppressed only with great difficulty and at considerable cost to the imperial treasury.
key_figures: Jiaqing Emperor (Qing ruler during the rebellion)
Wang Lun (early rebellion leader, executed 1774, but his followers continue)

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military reports
Chinese historical chronicles and official histories

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the White Lotus Rebellion in 1796 is well-documented in Qing records. The date and initial spread are uncontested.
cross_references: Follows from 1796-010 (Qianlong's abdication and transition of power)
Led to 1804-001 (Suppression of the White Lotus Rebellion)

events.title: Treaty of San Ildefonso between Spain and France
events.description: On 19 August 1796, Spain and France sign the Treaty of San Ildefonso, a secret alliance directed against Britain. The treaty commits Spain to support France in its wars against Britain and provides for the mutual defense of their colonial possessions. The alliance is partly motivated by Spain's desire to recover Gibraltar and other territories lost to Britain. However, the alliance proves costly for Spain, as it leads to increased British naval attacks on Spanish shipping and colonies. The treaty represents a shift in Spanish foreign policy away from traditional alliances and toward dependence on France, a relationship that will dominate Spanish politics for the next two decades.

sources.name: Treaty text and official Spanish and French records

events.certainty_note: The treaty and its date are well-documented in Spanish and French archives. The terms and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to increased Anglo-Spanish naval conflict

events.title: Mungo Park's exploration of the Niger River
events.description: Scottish explorer Mungo Park, sponsored by the African Association, undertakes an expedition to explore the Niger River in West Africa. Park travels from the Gambia River inland, reaching the Niger River in July 1796 and following it downstream for several hundred miles. His journey provides the first detailed European account of the Niger's course and the interior of West Africa. Park's expedition is significant for European geographical knowledge of Africa, though it also marks the beginning of European penetration into the interior that will lead to colonization. Park's account, published in 1799 as 'Travels in the Interior Districts of Africa,' becomes influential in shaping European perceptions of Africa.
key_figures: Ismail, King of Kasson (local ruler who assisted Park)
Joseph Renaud (French trader who provided information)

sources.name: Mungo Park's journal and later published account 'Travels in the Interior Districts of Africa' (1799)
Contemporary European geographical and travel literature

events.certainty_note: Park's expedition and the dates are well-documented in his journal and published account. The route and discoveries are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader European exploration of Africa

events.title: Russo-Persian War: Russian expansion in the Caucasus
events.description: In 1796, Russian forces under General Valerian Zubov conduct a campaign in the Caucasus and Persian territories, capturing the fortress of Derbent and advancing into present-day Azerbaijan. This campaign is part of Catherine II's broader strategy to expand Russian influence in the Caucasus and weaken Persian power. The campaign results in the temporary Russian occupation of several Persian territories, though these are largely abandoned after Catherine's death in 1796. The campaign demonstrates Russian military superiority in the region and sets the stage for the Russo-Persian Wars of the 19th century.
key_figures: Empress Catherine II (Russian ruler, dies November 1796)
events.certainty_note: The Russian campaign in 1796 is well-documented in Russian military records. The dates and outcomes are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader Russian expansion in the Caucasus
Led to 1797-003 (Abandonment of Persian territories under Paul I)

events.title: Death of Empress Catherine II of Russia
events.description: On 17 November 1796 (5 November in the Julian calendar used in Russia), Empress Catherine II of Russia dies at age 67 after a reign of 34 years. She is succeeded by her son, Paul I, whose reign marks a sharp reversal of many of Catherine's policies. Catherine's death ends the era of enlightened absolutism in Russia and the period of Russian expansion under her rule. Paul I's accession leads to the abandonment of the Persian campaign, a reorientation of foreign policy, and significant domestic changes. Catherine's reign is remembered as a period of Russian cultural flourishing, territorial expansion, and the consolidation of serfdom.

sources.name: Russian court records and official announcements

events.certainty_note: The date of Catherine's death is well-established in Russian records. The date varies between the Julian calendar (5 November) and the Gregorian calendar (17 November) used in Western Europe.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements: (1) Napoleon's Italian Campaign is sometimes dated as beginning in April 1796, but some sources place initial operations in March 1796. The significance attributed to the campaign as establishing Napoleon's reputation is generally accepted, but some historians argue that his later Egyptian campaign (1798-1799) was equally or more significant in shaping European perceptions. (2) Jenner's vaccination experiment is sometimes cited as occurring in 1796, but some sources place the first successful vaccination in May 1796 and subsequent confirmatory experiments in 1797-1798. The exact date of the first vaccination (14 May 1796) is well-established, but the significance attributed to it as the 'birth of vaccination' is somewhat anachronistic—variolation (inoculation with smallpox material) had been practiced for centuries. (3) The White Lotus Rebellion is sometimes dated as beginning in 1795 rather than 1796, with different sources citing different initial uprisings. The rebellion was not a single coordinated event but a series of uprisings that coalesced over time. (4) Tipu Sultan's resistance to British expansion is well-documented, but the attribution of his eventual fall to 1796 events is somewhat speculative—the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War did not begin until 1799. (5) The Treaty of San Ildefonso is sometimes cited as a secret treaty, but the extent of its secrecy and the precise terms are debated by historians. Some sources suggest that Britain was aware of the treaty's existence, if not its full terms.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1796 is rich and well-documented, particularly for Europe and the Americas. European sources (British, French, Spanish, Russian) are abundant and detailed. Asian sources (Chinese, Persian, Indian) are also available but are often filtered through European interpretations and colonial-era scholarship. African sources are sparse, with most information about Mungo Park's expedition coming from his own account rather than from African perspectives. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are less well-represented. There is a significant bias toward 'great men' and elite decision-makers; the experiences of ordinary people, enslaved persons, and marginalized groups are largely absent from the historical record. Colonial-era scholarship on India and Africa often reflects European assumptions about 'progress' and 'civilization' that modern historians have critiqued. The sources for 1796 are generally reliable, but they reflect the perspectives and biases of their authors and the societies that produced them.

graph_edges.note: Napoleon's Italian Campaign led directly to the Treaty of Campo Formio in 1797
Jenner's vaccination experiment led to the publication of his findings in 1798
Washington's Farewell Address directly preceded the 1796 presidential election
Qianlong's abdication and the transition of power contributed to the conditions that sparked the White Lotus Rebellion
Catherine II's death led to Paul I's accession and a reversal of Russian foreign policy
The Treaty of San Ildefonso established the Franco-Spanish alliance that would lead to the Peninsular War in 1808

era_context: The French Revolution is in its radical phase, with the Reign of Terror having ended in July 1794. The French Republic is consolidating power under the Directory (1795-1799). Europe is experiencing revolutionary upheaval and counter-revolutionary responses. The Industrial Revolution continues to accelerate in Britain. The Americas are establishing new republics and consolidating independence.

events.title: Treaty of Basel between France and Prussia
events.description: On 5 April 1795, France and Prussia sign the Treaty of Basel, ending Prussian participation in the War of the First Coalition against France. The treaty recognizes French control of the left bank of the Rhine and provides for Prussian neutrality in the ongoing war. The treaty is a significant diplomatic victory for France, as it removes one of the major powers opposing the Revolution. Prussia's withdrawal from the coalition reflects the exhaustion of the Prussian treasury and the desire of King Frederick William II to avoid further military commitments. The treaty effectively splits the anti-French coalition and allows France to concentrate its forces against Austria and other remaining enemies.
key_figures: Prussian diplomat Karl August von Hardenberg

sources.name: Treaty text and official French and Prussian records

events.certainty_note: The treaty and its date are well-documented in French and Prussian archives. The terms and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)
Led to 1795-002 (Treaty of Basel with Spain)

events.title: Treaty of Basel between France and Spain
events.description: On 22 July 1795, France and Spain sign the Treaty of Basel, ending Spanish participation in the War of the First Coalition. The treaty provides for the cession of the Spanish portion of Santo Domingo (present-day Dominican Republic) to France and the restoration of Spanish territories captured by France. The treaty reflects Spain's military exhaustion and the desire of King Charles IV to end the costly war. Like the Prussian treaty, the Spanish treaty further weakens the anti-French coalition. However, the treaty also sets the stage for the Franco-Spanish alliance of 1796, as Spain seeks French support against Britain.

sources.name: Treaty text and official French and Spanish records

events.certainty_note: The treaty and its date are well-documented in French and Spanish archives. The terms and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: Partitions of Poland: Third Partition completed
events.description: In 1795, the Third Partition of Poland is completed, with Russia, Prussia, and Austria dividing the remaining Polish territories among themselves. The final partition, signed in October 1795, eliminates Poland as an independent state. The partitions (1772, 1793, 1795) represent one of the most significant territorial redistributions of the 18th century and demonstrate the power of the great powers to reshape the map of Europe. Poland ceases to exist as a sovereign state until 1918. The partitions are condemned by Polish patriots and become a rallying point for Polish nationalism throughout the 19th century.

sources.name: Treaty texts and official Russian, Prussian, and Austrian records
Russian, Prussian, and Austrian diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The Third Partition of Poland in 1795 is well-documented in Russian, Prussian, and Austrian archives. The date and terms are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader history of Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (1569-1795)

events.title: Reign of Terror ends; Thermidorian Reaction consolidates
events.description: By 1795, the Thermidorian Reaction (which began in July 1794 with the fall of Robespierre) has consolidated its control over the French government. The radical phase of the Revolution has ended, and the Directory (1795-1799) is establishing a more conservative, bourgeois republic. The execution of Robespierre and the closure of the Committee of Public Safety mark the end of the Terror. In 1795, the Directory suppresses both radical Jacobin uprisings (the Conspiracy of the Equals, led by Gracchus Babeuf) and royalist uprisings (the Vendée uprising). The Directory's consolidation represents a shift from revolutionary idealism toward pragmatic governance and the protection of property rights.

sources.name: French government records and Directory documents
Personal correspondence and memoirs of Directory members

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of the Thermidorian Reaction by 1795 is well-documented in French government records and contemporary sources. The date and significance are uncontested.

events.title: British naval victory at the Glorious First of June (1794) and continued naval dominance
events.description: By 1795, the British Royal Navy has consolidated its dominance following the Glorious First of June (1794), a major naval battle against the French. In 1795, the British continue to dominate the seas, protecting British trade and threatening French commerce. The British navy's superiority is reflected in the capture of French colonies and the disruption of French trade. This naval dominance is a crucial factor in Britain's ability to sustain the war against France despite the military successes of the French army on land. The naval dominance also allows Britain to project power globally and to protect its expanding colonial empire.

sources.name: British Admiralty records and official dispatches

events.certainty_note: British naval dominance in 1795 is well-documented in Admiralty records. The strategic significance is uncontested.
cross_references: Led to continued British naval supremacy throughout the Napoleonic Wars

events.title: Jay Treaty between the United States and Britain
events.description: On 19 November 1794, the Jay Treaty is signed between the United States and Britain (ratified in 1795). The treaty, negotiated by Chief Justice John Jay, resolves several outstanding disputes between the two nations, including British impressment of American sailors, British support for Native American resistance to American expansion, and trade issues. The treaty is controversial in the United States, with many viewing it as too favorable to Britain. However, it averts a potential war between the two nations and establishes a framework for peaceful coexistence. The treaty also opens British markets to American trade and provides for the evacuation of British troops from American territory. The treaty is ratified by the Senate in June 1795.

sources.name: Treaty text and official U.S. and British records
U.S. Senate records and ratification documents
Contemporary American newspapers and political writings

events.certainty_note: The Jay Treaty and its ratification in 1795 are well-documented in U.S. and British archives. The date and terms are uncontested.

events.title: Kościuszko Uprising in Poland
events.description: In 1794 (not 1795, but the aftermath continues into 1795), Polish general Tadeusz Kościuszko leads an uprising against Russian and Prussian occupation. The uprising, which begins in March 1794, is suppressed by October 1794, but its aftermath and the memory of the uprising dominate Polish consciousness in 1795. Kościuszko is captured and imprisoned, and the uprising's failure contributes to the final partition of Poland in 1795. The uprising represents the last major Polish resistance to partition and becomes a symbol of Polish nationalism and independence.
key_figures: Tadeusz Kościuszko (Polish general and uprising leader)
events.certainty_note: The Kościuszko Uprising occurred in 1794, but its aftermath and significance extend into 1795. The dates and outcomes are well-documented.

events.title: British capture of the Cape of Good Hope (preliminary operations)
events.region: Cape of Good Hope, South Africa
events.description: In 1795, British naval forces begin operations against the Dutch-held Cape of Good Hope. The British, seeking to deny the French access to this strategic point and to secure their own eastern trade routes, dispatch an expedition. The actual capture occurs in September 1796, but the planning and preliminary operations occur in 1795. The British recognize the strategic importance of the Cape as a supply station on the route to Asia and as a base for operations in the Indian Ocean.
key_figures: Admiral Sir George Elphinstone (British naval commander)
General James Henry Craig (British military commander)

sources.name: British Admiralty and War Office records

events.certainty_note: The planning and preliminary operations for the capture of the Cape occur in 1795, but the actual capture occurs in 1796. The dates are somewhat fluid depending on the source.
cross_references: Led to 1796-004 (British capture of the Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Qianlong Emperor's reign continues
events.description: In 1795, the Qianlong Emperor continues his reign over the Qing Dynasty. By 1795, Qianlong has ruled for 60 years and is in his 85th year of life. He is beginning to prepare for his abdication, which will occur in 1796. The Qing Dynasty is at the height of its territorial extent, having conquered Tibet, Xinjiang, and Mongolia. However, by 1795, signs of decline are beginning to appear: corruption in the bureaucracy, the rise of the eunuch Heshen, and growing social unrest. The Qing economy is beginning to show signs of strain, and the population is growing faster than agricultural production.

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Qing Shilu) and court documents
Chinese historical chronicles and official histories
Contemporary European diplomatic reports from China

events.certainty_note: The continuation of Qianlong's reign in 1795 is well-documented in Qing imperial records. The date and circumstances are uncontested.

events.title: British East India Company consolidates control in India
events.description: By 1795, the British East India Company has consolidated its control over much of India, particularly in Bengal and the Madras Presidency. The Company's military victories over Indian rulers and French competitors have established British dominance. In 1795, the Company is expanding its administrative control and extracting revenue from Indian territories. The Company's rule is characterized by the subordination of Indian interests to British commercial interests and the extraction of wealth from India. The Company's expansion sets the stage for the formal British Raj, which will be established in 1858.

sources.name: British East India Company records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of British East India Company control in 1795 is well-documented in Company records and British government correspondence. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader British expansion in India

events.title: Haitian Revolution: Toussaint Louverture consolidates power
events.description: In 1795, Toussaint Louverture, a former enslaved person who has risen to prominence in the Haitian Revolution, consolidates his power over the island of Saint-Domingue (present-day Haiti). The Haitian Revolution, which began in 1791, has resulted in the abolition of slavery and the establishment of a Black republic. By 1795, Toussaint has emerged as the dominant military and political leader. He unifies the various factions of the revolution and begins to establish a stable government. Toussaint's rise represents one of the most significant challenges to European colonialism and slavery in the Americas.
key_figures: Toussaint Louverture (Haitian military and political leader)
events.certainty_note: Toussaint Louverture's consolidation of power in 1795 is well-documented in French colonial records and contemporary accounts. The date and significance are uncontested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Russian advances continue
events.description: In 1795, Russian forces continue their advances against the Ottoman Empire, particularly in the Caucasus region. The Russo-Turkish Wars (1768-1774, 1787-1792) have established Russian dominance in the region. By 1795, Russia is consolidating its control over the northern Caucasus and the Black Sea region. The Ottoman Empire is in decline, and Russia is the ascendant power in the region. These conflicts set the stage for the 19th-century 'Eastern Question,' which will dominate European diplomacy.
key_figures: General Grigory Potemkin (deceased 1791, but his legacy continues)
events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Russian-Ottoman conflict in 1795 are well-documented, though specific battles and dates may vary slightly in different sources.
cross_references: Led to Russian consolidation in the Caucasus

events.title: Whiskey Rebellion: Federal suppression and consolidation of U.S. authority
events.description: In 1795, the aftermath of the Whiskey Rebellion (1794) is being consolidated. Federal troops, having suppressed the rebellion in 1794, remain in the region to enforce federal authority. The rebellion's suppression demonstrates the federal government's ability to enforce its laws and taxation. By 1795, the federal government has successfully established its authority over the frontier regions. The rebellion's suppression marks a turning point in American federalism, establishing the principle that the federal government, not state governments or local militias, holds ultimate coercive power.
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of federal authority following the rebellion is well-documented. 1795 marks the point at which the rebellion's lessons had been absorbed into American political practice.
cross_references: Led to 1796-005 (Consolidation of U.S. federal authority)

events.title: British capture of French colonies in the Caribbean
events.description: In 1795, British naval and military forces capture several French colonies in the Caribbean, including Guadeloupe and Martinique. These captures are part of the broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797) and reflect British naval dominance. The captures disrupt French trade and weaken French colonial power. However, many of these colonies are returned to France in subsequent peace treaties. The captures demonstrate the importance of naval power in colonial conflicts and the vulnerability of French colonies to British attack.

sources.name: British Admiralty and War Office records

events.certainty_note: The British captures of French colonies in 1795 are well-documented in British and French records. The dates and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: Mungo Park's exploration preparations and early travels
events.description: In 1795, Scottish explorer Mungo Park, sponsored by the African Association, begins his preparations for his expedition to explore the Niger River in West Africa. Park travels from the Gambia River inland in 1795, beginning his journey that will lead to the discovery of the Niger River in July 1796. Park's expedition is significant for European geographical knowledge of Africa and marks the beginning of European penetration into the interior that will lead to colonization.

sources.name: Mungo Park's journal and later published account 'Travels in the Interior Districts of Africa' (1799)
Contemporary European geographical and travel literature

events.certainty_note: Park's expedition and the dates are well-documented in his journal and published account. The route and discoveries are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to 1796-013 (Mungo Park reaches the Niger River)

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements: (1) The Treaty of Basel with Prussia is sometimes dated as 5 April 1795, but some sources cite different dates depending on the calendar system used (Julian vs. Gregorian). The significance of the treaty in weakening the anti-French coalition is generally accepted, but some historians argue that Prussia's withdrawal was inevitable due to financial exhaustion rather than French diplomatic skill. (2) The Treaty of Basel with Spain is sometimes dated as 22 July 1795, but the exact date varies in different sources. The treaty's significance in ending Spanish participation in the war is uncontested, but the terms regarding Santo Domingo are sometimes disputed. (3) The Third Partition of Poland is sometimes dated as occurring in 1795, but the exact date of the final partition agreement varies in different sources (some cite October 1795, others cite different dates). The fact of the partition is uncontested, but the precise date is somewhat fluid. (4) The Kościuszko Uprising occurred in 1794, not 1795, but its aftermath and significance extend into 1795. The inclusion of this event in 1795 is somewhat anachronistic. (5) The British capture of the Cape of Good Hope is sometimes dated as occurring in 1795, but the actual capture occurred in September 1796. The planning and preliminary operations occurred in 1795, but the actual capture is a 1796 event.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1795 is rich and well-documented, particularly for Europe and the Americas. European sources (British, French, Spanish, Russian, Prussian) are abundant and detailed. Asian sources (Chinese, Indian) are also available but are often filtered through European interpretations. African sources are sparse, with most information about Mungo Park's expedition coming from his own account rather than from African perspectives. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are less well-represented. There is a significant bias toward 'great men' and elite decision-makers; the experiences of ordinary people, enslaved persons, and marginalized groups are largely absent from the historical record. Colonial-era scholarship on India and Africa often reflects European assumptions about 'progress' and 'civilization' that modern historians have critiqued. The sources for 1795 are generally reliable, but they reflect the perspectives and biases of their authors and the societies that produced them.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Basel with Prussia and the Treaty of Basel with Spain were signed in the same year and reflected similar strategic dynamics
The Third Partition of Poland followed the failure of the Kościuszko Uprising
The consolidation of the Thermidorian Reaction and British naval dominance were contemporary developments
The Jay Treaty's ratification in 1795 set the stage for the 1796 presidential election
Toussaint Louverture's consolidation of power in 1795 led to Haitian independence in 1804

era_context: The French Revolution is in its most radical phase, with the Reign of Terror (1793-1794) reaching its peak. The Committee of Public Safety, dominated by Robespierre, is executing thousands of perceived enemies of the Revolution. The War of the First Coalition (1792-1797) continues, with France fighting against a coalition of European monarchies. The Industrial Revolution is accelerating in Britain. The Americas are consolidating independence and establishing new republics.

events.title: Fall of Robespierre and the end of the Reign of Terror
events.description: On 28 July 1794 (10 Thermidor in the Revolutionary calendar), Maximilien Robespierre and his allies are arrested and executed. The fall of Robespierre marks the end of the Reign of Terror, a period of radical revolutionary violence that lasted from 1793 to 1794. During the Terror, thousands of people were executed by guillotine, including King Louis XVI (January 1793), Queen Marie Antoinette (October 1793), and numerous political opponents. Robespierre's fall is precipitated by a coalition of moderate revolutionaries and radical Jacobins who fear his growing power. The execution of Robespierre and his allies marks a turning point in the French Revolution, ending the radical phase and beginning the more conservative Thermidorian Reaction.

sources.name: French government records and Committee of Public Safety documents
Personal correspondence and memoirs of revolutionaries

events.certainty_note: The date of Robespierre's fall (28 July 1794 / 10 Thermidor) is well-established in French government records and contemporary sources. The event is one of the most thoroughly documented moments in the French Revolution.

events.title: Glorious First of June: British naval victory over France
events.region: Atlantic Ocean, off the coast of France
events.description: On 1 June 1794, a British fleet under Admiral Lord Howe defeats a French fleet in a major naval battle off the coast of France. The battle, known as the Glorious First of June, results in the capture of six French ships and the death of thousands of French sailors. The British victory is significant for establishing British naval dominance during the War of the First Coalition. Although the French fleet escapes destruction, the British victory demonstrates the superiority of British naval tactics and seamanship. The battle boosts British morale and establishes the Royal Navy as the dominant naval power in Europe.

sources.name: British Admiralty records and official dispatches

events.certainty_note: The date of the Glorious First of June (1 June 1794) is well-established in British and French naval records. The outcome and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: Whiskey Rebellion in the United States
events.description: In 1794, a rebellion erupts in western Pennsylvania and surrounding regions in response to the federal excise tax on whiskey, imposed by the federal government under Secretary of Treasury Alexander Hamilton. The rebellion, known as the Whiskey Rebellion, involves armed resistance to federal tax collectors. In August 1794, President George Washington dispatches federal troops to suppress the rebellion. The federal troops, numbering around 13,000, march into Pennsylvania and suppress the rebellion without significant bloodshed. The suppression of the rebellion demonstrates the federal government's ability to enforce its laws and taxation, establishing the principle that the federal government, not state governments or local militias, holds ultimate coercive power.
events.certainty_note: The Whiskey Rebellion and its suppression in 1794 are well-documented in federal records and contemporary sources. The dates and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader development of U.S. federalism

events.title: Battle of Fleurus: French victory in the War of the First Coalition
events.description: On 26 June 1794, French forces under General Jean-Baptiste Jourdan defeat an Austrian army at the Battle of Fleurus in Belgium. The French victory is significant for establishing French military dominance in the Low Countries and for demonstrating the effectiveness of the French Revolutionary army. The battle is notable for the use of a hot air balloon for reconnaissance, marking one of the first military uses of aerial observation. The French victory at Fleurus leads to the conquest of the Austrian Netherlands (present-day Belgium) and the establishment of French control over the region.
events.certainty_note: The date of the Battle of Fleurus (26 June 1794) is well-established in French and Austrian military records. The outcome and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)
Led to French conquest of the Austrian Netherlands

events.title: Haitian Revolution: Abolition of slavery declared
events.description: In 1794, the French National Convention formally abolishes slavery throughout the French colonies, including Saint-Domingue (present-day Haiti). The abolition is partly motivated by the need to gain the support of enslaved people in the Haitian Revolution and partly by revolutionary ideals of liberty and equality. The abolition of slavery in Saint-Domingue is significant for establishing the first Black republic in the Americas. However, the abolition is not universally accepted, and slavery is later reinstated by Napoleon in 1802. The abolition of slavery in 1794 marks a turning point in the Haitian Revolution and establishes the principle that enslaved people can fight for and achieve their own freedom.

sources.name: French National Convention records and decrees

events.certainty_note: The abolition of slavery by the French National Convention in 1794 is well-documented in French government records. The date and significance are uncontested.

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Qianlong Emperor's reign continues
events.description: In 1794, the Qianlong Emperor continues his reign over the Qing Dynasty. By 1794, Qianlong has ruled for 59 years and is in his 84th year of life. The Qing Dynasty is at the height of its territorial extent, having conquered Tibet, Xinjiang, and Mongolia. However, by 1794, signs of decline are beginning to appear: corruption in the bureaucracy, the rise of the eunuch Heshen, and growing social unrest. The Qing economy is beginning to show signs of strain, and the population is growing faster than agricultural production. The White Lotus Rebellion, which will erupt in 1796, is being prepared by growing social discontent.

sources.name: Qing imperial records (Qing Shilu) and court documents
Chinese historical chronicles and official histories
Contemporary European diplomatic reports from China

events.certainty_note: The continuation of Qianlong's reign in 1794 is well-documented in Qing imperial records. The date and circumstances are uncontested.

events.title: British East India Company consolidates control in India
events.description: By 1794, the British East India Company has consolidated its control over much of India, particularly in Bengal and the Madras Presidency. The Company's military victories over Indian rulers and French competitors have established British dominance. In 1794, the Company is expanding its administrative control and extracting revenue from Indian territories. The Company's rule is characterized by the subordination of Indian interests to British commercial interests and the extraction of wealth from India. The Company's expansion sets the stage for the formal British Raj, which will be established in 1858.

sources.name: British East India Company records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of British East India Company control in 1794 is well-documented in Company records and British government correspondence. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader British expansion in India

events.title: Kościuszko Uprising in Poland
events.description: In March 1794, Polish general Tadeusz Kościuszko leads an uprising against Russian and Prussian occupation. The uprising is motivated by Polish nationalism and the desire to restore Polish independence. Kościuszko's forces achieve initial successes, defeating Russian and Prussian forces in several engagements. However, by October 1794, the uprising is suppressed by Russian forces under General Aleksandr Suvorov. Kościuszko is captured and imprisoned. The failure of the uprising contributes to the final partition of Poland in 1795. The uprising represents the last major Polish resistance to partition and becomes a symbol of Polish nationalism and independence.
key_figures: Tadeusz Kościuszko (Polish general and uprising leader)
events.certainty_note: The Kościuszko Uprising in 1794 is well-documented in Russian and Polish records. The dates and outcomes are uncontested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Russian advances continue
events.description: In 1794, Russian forces continue their advances against the Ottoman Empire, particularly in the Caucasus region. The Russo-Turkish Wars (1768-1774, 1787-1792) have established Russian dominance in the region. By 1794, Russia is consolidating its control over the northern Caucasus and the Black Sea region. The Ottoman Empire is in decline, and Russia is the ascendant power in the region. These conflicts set the stage for the 19th-century 'Eastern Question,' which will dominate European diplomacy.
key_figures: General Grigory Potemkin (deceased 1791, but his legacy continues)
events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Russian-Ottoman conflict in 1794 are well-documented, though specific battles and dates may vary slightly in different sources.
cross_references: Led to Russian consolidation in the Caucasus

events.title: French conquest of the Austrian Netherlands
events.description: In 1794, following the French victory at Fleurus, French forces complete the conquest of the Austrian Netherlands (present-day Belgium). The French establish control over the region and begin to incorporate it into the French Republic. The conquest of the Austrian Netherlands is significant for establishing French dominance in the Low Countries and for demonstrating the effectiveness of the French Revolutionary army. The region remains under French control until 1815, when it is transferred to the Netherlands following the Congress of Vienna.
events.certainty_note: The French conquest of the Austrian Netherlands in 1794 is well-documented in French and Austrian military records. The dates and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: Jay Treaty negotiations between the United States and Britain
events.description: In 1794, Chief Justice John Jay is sent to Britain to negotiate a treaty resolving outstanding disputes between the United States and Britain. The negotiations, which occur in 1794, result in the Jay Treaty, signed on 19 November 1794. The treaty resolves several outstanding disputes, including British impressment of American sailors, British support for Native American resistance to American expansion, and trade issues. The treaty is controversial in the United States, with many viewing it as too favorable to Britain. However, it averts a potential war between the two nations and establishes a framework for peaceful coexistence.
events.certainty_note: The Jay Treaty negotiations in 1794 are well-documented in U.S. and British records. The date and significance are uncontested.

events.title: Tipu Sultan's resistance to British expansion in India
events.description: In 1794, Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore, continues his resistance to British expansion in southern India. Tipu, who has inherited the throne from his father Hyder Ali, is attempting to modernize Mysore's military and to resist British encroachment. In 1794, Tipu is consolidating his power and seeking alliances with other Indian rulers and with France. The Third Anglo-Mysore War (1790-1792) has recently ended, and Tipu is preparing for future conflicts. Tipu's resistance to British expansion represents one of the last major challenges to British dominance in southern India.

sources.name: British East India Company records and military dispatches

events.certainty_note: Tipu Sultan's resistance to British expansion in 1794 is well-documented in British East India Company records and contemporary accounts. The date and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Led to 1799-001 (Fourth Anglo-Mysore War and Tipu Sultan's death)

events.title: Reign of Terror: Execution of Marie Antoinette
events.description: On 16 October 1793 (not 1794, but the aftermath continues into 1794), Queen Marie Antoinette is executed by guillotine during the Reign of Terror. However, the execution of Marie Antoinette and other members of the royal family continues to dominate French politics in 1794. The execution of the Queen is significant for symbolizing the complete break with the old regime and for demonstrating the radical nature of the Revolution. The execution of Marie Antoinette is followed by the execution of thousands of other perceived enemies of the Revolution during the Reign of Terror.

sources.name: French government records and Committee of Public Safety documents
Personal correspondence and memoirs of revolutionaries

events.certainty_note: The execution of Marie Antoinette occurred on 16 October 1793, not 1794. However, the aftermath and significance of the execution extend into 1794. The date of the execution is well-established.
cross_references: Follows from 1793-002 (Execution of King Louis XVI)

events.title: British naval dominance in the Caribbean
events.description: In 1794, British naval forces continue to dominate the Caribbean, capturing French colonies and disrupting French trade. The British naval dominance is reflected in the capture of French colonies such as Guadeloupe and Martinique. The British naval dominance in the Caribbean is part of the broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797) and reflects British naval superiority. The captures disrupt French trade and weaken French colonial power. However, many of these colonies are returned to France in subsequent peace treaties.

sources.name: British Admiralty and War Office records

events.certainty_note: British naval dominance in the Caribbean in 1794 is well-documented in British and French records. The dates and significance are uncontested.
cross_references: Part of broader War of the First Coalition (1792-1797)

events.title: Whiskey Rebellion: Federal suppression and consolidation of U.S. authority
events.description: In August 1794, President George Washington dispatches federal troops to suppress the Whiskey Rebellion in western Pennsylvania. The federal troops, numbering around 13,000, march into Pennsylvania and suppress the rebellion without significant bloodshed. The suppression of the rebellion demonstrates the federal government's ability to enforce its laws and taxation. The rebellion's suppression marks a turning point in American federalism, establishing the principle that the federal government, not state governments or local militias, holds ultimate coercive power. The successful suppression of the rebellion establishes Washington's authority and the legitimacy of the federal government.
events.certainty_note: The suppression of the Whiskey Rebellion in August 1794 is well-documented in federal records and contemporary sources. The dates and significance are uncontested.

The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are less well-represented.
